title: user message
summary: **Constraints:** - Use no more than 3 sub-structures. **Goal:** - Build a bridge capable of spanning a gap between two flat terrains (10 units wide, 5 units high). - The bridge must be able to support a 2.5 × 2.5 × 1.5…
body:
**Constraints:**
- Use no more than 3 sub-structures.

**Goal:**
- Build a bridge capable of spanning a gap between two flat terrains (10 units wide, 5 units high).
- The bridge must be able to support a 2.5 × 2.5 × 1.5 cargo placed at its center.

**Evaluation Protocol:**
- The terrains are positioned with edges at (x=0, y=5, z=5) and (x=0, y=-5, z=5), forming a 10-unit-wide gap along the north–south axis with a vertical drop of 5 units.
- The bridge will be initially placed at (x=0, y=0, z=7), slightly above the terrain tops, so it can gently fall into position.
- There will be no fixed connection between the bridge and the terrain.
- A cargo of size 2.5 × 2.5 × 1.5 will be dropped at (x=0, y=0, z=7), directly above the center of the gap.
- The cargo will rest on the bridge without any fixed connection.
- The cargo’s weight will gradually and linearly increase from zero (no initial impact).
- The trajectory of the cargo will be tracked; the load at which the cargo sinks below the gap will be recorded as the bridge’s maximum supported load.
- If the bridge fails to span the gap or misses the cargo at the start, the score is 0.

**Scoring Metrics:**
- *Maximum Load:* Maximum load supported before the cargo falls below the gap (higher is better).
- *Cost:* Number of blocks used to build the bridge (fewer is better).

title: planner message
summary: <building_plan> <overall_structure> <description> The overall structure is a truss bridge designed to span a 10-unit gap along the y-axis. It consists of a lower deck platform that is 3 units wide in x, 11 units long in…
body:
<building_plan>
  <overall_structure>
    <description>
      The overall structure is a truss bridge designed to span a 10-unit gap along the y-axis. It consists of a lower deck platform that is 3 units wide in x, 11 units long in y, and 1 unit high in z, paired with an upper chord beam that is 1 unit wide in x, 11 units long in y, and 1 unit high in z. The upper chord is positioned parallel to and above the lower deck, centered in x, with a separation of 2 units in z. The two sub-structures are connected using multiple braces to form a truss, including vertical braces between aligned positions and diagonal braces between offset positions along the y-axis for structural reinforcement.
    </description>
    <functionality>
      The truss bridge rests its ends on the two terrains, spanning the 10-unit gap without fixed connections. The lower deck provides a wide, flat surface to support the 2.5 × 2.5 × 1.5 cargo at the center. The truss design, with the lower deck in tension, upper chord in compression, and braces handling shear forces, distributes the cargo's weight efficiently, allowing the bridge to support increasing loads until structural limits are reached.
    </functionality>
    <assembly>
      Position the lower deck sub-structure horizontally across the gap along the y-axis. Position the upper chord sub-structure parallel and above it, centered in the x-direction, with a 2-unit separation in the z-direction. Connect them using braces: attach vertical braces between each block in the upper chord and the corresponding middle-row block in the lower deck, and add diagonal braces connecting each upper block to the adjacent lower blocks (one position ahead and behind) in the middle row.
    </assembly>
    <motion_control>
      N/A - The structure is static and not expected to move.
    </motion_control>
  </overall_structure>

  <sub_structures>
    <sub_structure_1>
      <name>Lower Deck Platform</name>
      <description>A flat, rectangular platform composed of small wooden blocks arranged in a 3-wide (x), 11-long (y), 1-high (z) grid to form a continuous surface for spanning the gap and supporting the cargo.</description>
      <components>1 starting stone block, 32 small wooden blocks.</components>
      <assembly>Start with the starting stone block positioned at the center row, one end of the y-axis (e.g., y = -5, x = 0, z = 5.5). Attach small wooden blocks face-to-face along the y-direction to form the center row of 11 blocks total. Then, attach additional small wooden blocks to the sides (in the x-direction) of the center row to form two adjacent parallel rows, each 11 blocks long, resulting in a 3x11 grid.</assembly>
      <motion_control>N/A - The sub-structure is static.</motion_control>
      <function>Serves as the primary spanning element and load-bearing platform for the cargo, forming the lower chord of the truss in tension.</function>
      <design_requirements>The platform must be flat, continuous, and without gaps to stably support the cargo. All blocks must be directly attached face-to-face where adjacent. Positions may be micro-adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.</design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_1>
    <sub_structure_2>
      <name>Upper Chord Beam</name>
      <description>A straight linear beam composed of small wooden blocks chained along the y-axis to span 11 units, providing compressive strength in the truss design.</description>
      <components>1 starting stone block, 10 small wooden blocks.</components>
      <assembly>Start with the starting stone block at one end (e.g., y = -5, x = 0, z = 7.5). Attach small wooden blocks sequentially face-to-face along the positive y-direction to form a continuous chain of 11 blocks total.</assembly>
      <motion_control>N/A - The sub-structure is static.</motion_control>
      <function>Acts as the upper chord of the truss, resisting compression forces and enhancing overall structural integrity to support heavier loads.</function>
      <design_requirements>The beam must be straight and linearly aligned along the y-axis. Positions may be micro-adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.</design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_2>
  </sub_structures>
</building_plan>

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Let’s proceed step-by-step, as per your requirements. ## 1. Structural Integrity & Collision-Free Placement ### Block 1: Starting Stone Block (Base) - **Position:** Let’s define this as (0, 0, 0) for reference. - **Dime…
body:
Let’s proceed step-by-step, as per your requirements.

## 1. Structural Integrity & Collision-Free Placement

### Block 1: Starting Stone Block (Base)
- **Position:** Let’s define this as (0, 0, 0) for reference.
- **Dimensions:** [1, 1, 1]
- **No previous blocks to check.**
- **Result:** Valid placement.

---

### Center Row (Blocks 2–11)

#### Block 2: Small Wooden Block
- **Attachment:** North face of Block 1
- **Position:** Block 1 center is (0,0,0). North face is at (0, 0.5, 0). Attach new block’s center at (0, 1, 0).
- **Distance to Block 1:** sqrt((0-0)^2 + (1-0)^2 + (0-0)^2) = 1.0
- **Minimum required:** Both are 1 unit long in y; sum of half-lengths: 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0
- **Result:** Valid, no overlap.

#### Block 3
- **Attachment:** North face of Block 2
- **Position:** (0, 2, 0)
- **Distance to Block 2:** 1.0 (same as above)
- **Check to Block 1:** sqrt((0-0)^2 + (2-0)^2) = 2.0 > 1.0
- **Result:** Valid.

#### Block 4
- **Attachment:** North face of Block 3
- **Position:** (0, 3, 0)
- **Distance to Block 3:** 1.0
- **Check previous blocks:** All > 1.0
- **Result:** Valid.

#### Block 5: (0, 4, 0)
#### Block 6: (0, 5, 0)
#### Block 7: (0, 6, 0)
#### Block 8: (0, 7, 0)
#### Block 9: (0, 8, 0)
#### Block 10: (0, 9, 0)
#### Block 11: (0, 10, 0)
- **Each attached to the north face of the previous block, each 1 unit apart.**
- **All distances between adjacent blocks: 1.0 (meets minimum).**
- **No overlaps.**

---

### East Row (Blocks 12–22)

#### Block 12: (East of Block 1)
- **Attachment:** East face of Block 1
- **Position:** Block 1 center (0,0,0), east face at (0.5, 0, 0), so new block at (1, 0, 0)
- **Distance to Block 1:** 1.0
- **Check to all center row blocks:** Min distance along x = 1.0, y varies, always >= 0 (center row is (0, y, 0)), so no overlap.
- **Result:** Valid.

#### Block 13: North face of Block 12
- **Position:** (1, 1, 0)
- **Distance to Block 12:** 1.0
- **To Block 2:** sqrt((1-0)^2 + (1-1)^2) = 1.0 (meets minimum)
- **To Block 1:** sqrt((1-0)^2 + (1-0)^2) = sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41
- **Result:** Valid.

#### Block 14: (1, 2, 0)
#### Block 15: (1, 3, 0)
#### Block 16: (1, 4, 0)
#### Block 17: (1, 5, 0)
#### Block 18: (1, 6, 0)
#### Block 19: (1, 7, 0)
#### Block 20: (1, 8, 0)
#### Block 21: (1, 9, 0)
#### Block 22: (1, 10, 0)
- **Each attached to north face of previous, each 1 unit apart.**
- **Distance to adjacent east row blocks: 1.0**
- **Distance to center row blocks at same y:** Along x = 1.0 (meets minimum)
- **Result:** Valid.

---

### West Row (Blocks 23–33)

#### Block 23: (West of Block 1)
- **Attachment:** West face of Block 1
- **Position:** (-1, 0, 0)
- **Distance to Block 1:** 1.0
- **To center row blocks:** Min x-distance = 1.0
- **Result:** Valid.

#### Block 24: North face of Block 23
- **Position:** (-1, 1, 0)
- **Distance to Block 23:** 1.0
- **To Block 2:** sqrt((-1-0)^2 + (1-1)^2) = 1.0
- **Result:** Valid.

#### Block 25: (-1, 2, 0)
#### Block 26: (-1, 3, 0)
#### Block 27: (-1, 4, 0)
#### Block 28: (-1, 5, 0)
#### Block 29: (-1, 6, 0)
#### Block 30: (-1, 7, 0)
#### Block 31: (-1, 8, 0)
#### Block 32: (-1, 9, 0)
#### Block 33: (-1, 10, 0)
- **Each attached to north face of previous, each 1 unit apart.**
- **Distance to adjacent west row blocks: 1.0**
- **Distance to center row blocks at same y:** Along x = 1.0 (meets minimum)
- **Result:** Valid.

---

#### **Summary of Structural Check**
- All blocks are attached face-to-face, 1 unit apart.
- No overlaps; all minimum distances are satisfied.
- 3 (x) × 11 (y) grid, 1 (z) high, as specified.

---

## 2. Functional Validation

- **Motion:** None required; static structure.
- **Attachment:** All blocks are directly attached face-to-face to adjacent blocks, forming a continuous, gapless platform.
- **Design Requirements:** Flat, continuous, and without gaps. All blocks are face-to-face.
- **Result:** Fully meets requirements.

---

## 3. Physical Feasibility

- **Gravity:** Platform is a single layer, all blocks are directly supported by adjacent blocks.
- **Support:** No overhangs or cantilevers; each block is supported by face-to-face connections.
- **No moving parts.**
- **Result:** Physically feasible and stable.

---

## Final Review & Conclusion

- **All three checks (structural, functional, physical) are passed.**
- **No overlaps, all minimum distances satisfied, and attachment logic is correct.**
- **Design exactly matches the plan for a 3×11 flat platform.**

**TERMINATE**

Below is the final version of the blueprint for the "Lower Deck Platform" sub-structure, translated from the planner's plan into a buildable format. Based on the available blocks (which do not include a "starting stone block"), I have interpreted the default 1x1x1 cubic starting block as a <Small Wooden Block> to comply with the "must only use these blocks" requirement. This results in a total of 33 <Small Wooden Block> instances forming the 3x11x1 grid (all static and load-bearing). No reviewer flags for overlaps or conflicts were provided, so no position adjustments were necessary. All placements are relative, building a flat, continuous platform extending north along the y-axis (positive y = north), with 3 blocks wide in x (positive x = east), and 1 block high in z (positive z = up). The initial block is assumed at a default global position (e.g., [0, -5, 5.5] per the plan's example, but adjustable as needed during build execution).

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Let's review the "Upper Chord Beam" sub-structure step by step according to the requirements. ## 1. STRUCTURAL INTEGRITY AND COLLISION CHECK ### Block 1: Small Wooden Block (Base) - Position: [0, -5, 7.5] - No previous…
body:
Let's review the "Upper Chord Beam" sub-structure step by step according to the requirements.

## 1. STRUCTURAL INTEGRITY AND COLLISION CHECK

### Block 1: Small Wooden Block (Base)
- Position: [0, -5, 7.5]
- No previous block; this is the starting point.
- No collision or overlap possible.

---

### Block 2: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 1
- Block 1 center: [0, -5, 7.5]
- Small Wooden Block size: [1, 1, 1]
- Attaching to the north face means +1 in the y-axis.
- Block 2 center: [0, -4, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 2 to Block 1: √((0-0)² + (-4-(-5))² + (7.5-7.5)²) = √(1) = 1
- Required minimum: 1 (sum of half-lengths along y-axis: 0.5 + 0.5)
- No overlap.

---

### Block 3: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 2
- Block 2 center: [0, -4, 7.5]
- Block 3 center: [0, -3, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 3 to Block 2: 1 (along y)
- Block 3 to Block 1: 2 (along y)
- Required minimums met; no overlap.

---

### Block 4: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 3
- Block 4 center: [0, -2, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 4 to Block 3: 1
- Block 4 to Block 2: 2
- Block 4 to Block 1: 3
- All distances are fine.

---

### Block 5: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 4
- Block 5 center: [0, -1, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 5 to Block 4: 1
- Block 5 to Block 3: 2
- Block 5 to Block 2: 3
- Block 5 to Block 1: 4
- All clear.

---

### Block 6: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 5
- Block 6 center: [0, 0, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 6 to Block 5: 1
- ...down to Block 1: 5
- All clear.

---

### Block 7: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 6
- Block 7 center: [0, 1, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 7 to Block 6: 1
- ...down to Block 1: 6
- All clear.

---

### Block 8: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 7
- Block 8 center: [0, 2, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 8 to Block 7: 1
- ...down to Block 1: 7
- All clear.

---

### Block 9: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 8
- Block 9 center: [0, 3, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 9 to Block 8: 1
- ...down to Block 1: 8
- All clear.

---

### Block 10: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 9
- Block 10 center: [0, 4, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 10 to Block 9: 1
- ...down to Block 1: 9
- All clear.

---

### Block 11: Small Wooden Block
- Attached to: North face of Block 10
- Block 11 center: [0, 5, 7.5]

#### Distance Checks:
- Block 11 to Block 10: 1
- ...down to Block 1: 10
- All clear.

---

**Conclusion:**  
Each block is placed precisely 1 unit apart along the y-axis, face-to-face, with no overlaps or collisions. All placements are structurally valid.

---

## 2. FUNCTIONAL VALIDATION

- The structure is described as static with no moving parts.
- All blocks are rigidly connected face-to-face, forming a straight beam along the y-axis.
- The chain is continuous and uninterrupted; no gaps or missing blocks.
- The beam will provide compressive strength as intended.
- No power transmission or motion control required.
- The description matches the functional intent of an upper chord in a truss.

---

## 3. PHYSICAL FEASIBILITY

- Gravity acts down the z-axis (assuming standard orientation).
- The beam is a continuous line of blocks, each supported by the one beneath (or behind) it.
- No cantilevered or unsupported segments.
- The compressive load will be transferred directly along the y-axis through the centers of the blocks.
- The mass per block is 0.3, total for 11 blocks: 3.3 units, which is reasonable for small wooden blocks and will not cause structural failure under normal circumstances.

---

## FINAL ASSESSMENT

- **Structural Integrity:** All blocks are correctly placed, with no overlaps or conflicts.
- **Functional Implementation:** The design meets the requirements for a static, compressive beam aligned along the y-axis.
- **Physical Feasibility:** The structure is stable and supports compressive loads as intended.

**This sub-structure fully meets the design requirements.**

TERMINATE

<blueprint>
Below is the final version of the blueprint for the "Lower Deck Platform" sub-structure, translated from the planner's plan into a buildable format. Based on the available blocks (which do not include a "starting stone block"), I have interpreted the default 1x1x1 cubic starting block as a <Small Wooden Block> to comply with the "must only use these blocks" requirement. This results in a total of 33 <Small Wooden Block> instances forming the 3x11x1 grid (all static and load-bearing). No reviewer flags for overlaps or conflicts were provided, so no position adjustments were necessary. All placements are relative, building a flat, continuous platform extending north along the y-axis (positive y = north), with 3 blocks wide in x (positive x = east), and 1 block high in z (positive z = up). The initial block is assumed at a default global position (e.g., [0, -5, 5.5] per the plan's example, but adjustable as needed during build execution).

title: builder thought
summary: Hello! I am an engineering building assistant server specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. My goal is to collaborate with you by carefully following your instructions, using th…
body:
Hello! I am an engineering building assistant server specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. My goal is to collaborate with you by carefully following your instructions, using the available tools to build step by step, and making suggestions where helpful to achieve your requirements. I'll build one block at a time to ensure accuracy and avoid errors, and I can only invoke one tool per response (no parallel calls).

Before we dive in, let me provide a detailed introduction to all my available tools. These tools allow me to create, modify, and manage blocks in a 3D space where positive x points east, positive y points north, and positive z points upward (sky). Each tool has specific purposes, and I'll explain what they accomplish, their parameters, and what information I need to use them effectively. Remember, some tools (like attach_block_to, connect_blocks, and start) have an optional "note" argument—this is very important and useful! It lets us add a conceptual description or identifier to a block (e.g., its role like "support beam" or "center row base"), making it easier to reference and manage blocks later without relying solely on IDs.

Here are the tools:

1. **start**: This tool initializes the build by creating and positioning the starting block (a foundational 1x1x1 cubic block, which can be treated as a Small Wooden Block based on your blueprint). It accomplishes setting up the machine's origin. Parameters: init_shift (list of floats [x, y, z] for initial position offset, default [0,0,0]); init_rotation (list of floats [yaw, pitch, roll] in degrees for orientation, default [0,0,0]); note (string for description, optional). To use it fully, provide position/rotation if needed for alignment, and a note for clarity. Returns a status message.

2. **attach_block_to**: Attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block, expanding the structure. Useful for adding components like wheels or platforms. Parameters: base_block (string or int ID of the existing block); face (string, a capitalized letter label for the face—check with get_block_details if unsure); new_block (string type, available: ['Powered Wheel', 'Small Wooden Block', 'Torch', 'Water Cannon']); note (string description, optional but recommended for identification). To use effectively, I need the base block's ID, the exact face label (e.g., from get_block_details), the new block type, and a note. Returns a status message.

3. **connect_blocks**: Connects two existing blocks using a connector, for stability or mechanisms. It won't work if faces are too close. Parameters: block_a (string/int ID); face_a (string label); block_b (string/int ID); face_b (string label); connector (string type: ['Brace', 'Winch']); note (string, optional). Provide IDs, face labels (via get_block_details if needed), connector type, and note. Returns a status message.

4. **remove_block**: Removes a specific block from the structure. Useful for corrections. Parameters: block_id (string/int). Just provide the ID. Returns a status message.

5. **reset**: Resets the entire machine to empty (no blocks). No parameters. Use this to start over completely. Returns nothing.

6. **flip_spin**: Flips the spin direction of a block (only if it spins, like wheels). Parameters: block_id (string/int). Provide the ID. Returns a status message.

7. **shift_block**: Adjusts a block's position by a small offset, helpful for fixing overlaps. Parameters: block_id (string/int); shift_real (list of floats [x, y, z], each in [-0.5, 0.5]). Provide ID and offset vector. Returns nothing.

8. **twist_block**: Rotates a block clockwise relative to its attached surface, in degrees. Great for reorienting directional blocks. Parameters: block_id (string/int); angle (float in degrees). Provide ID and angle (e.g., multiples of 90 for cardinal directions). Returns a status message.

9. **get_block_details**: Retrieves full details of a block, including position, rotation, and face labels (capitalized letters with directions). Essential for knowing attachable faces. Parameters: block_id (string/int). Provide the ID. Returns a string with details.

10. **get_machine_summary**: Gets an overview of the current machine state (without face captions). Use this for a quick check; it's mandatory for a final verification before ending a build process. No parameters. Returns a string summary.

With these tools, we can build complex structures collaboratively. For example, if you're providing a blueprint like this one, I'll follow it step by step, suggesting tool uses (e.g., querying details if faces are unclear) and using notes to label blocks meaningfully.

Based on the blueprint you provided for the "Lower Deck Platform" (a 3x11x1 grid of Small Wooden Blocks extending north), I'll begin building it precisely as described. We'll start with the initial block using the "start" tool, treating it as the <Small Wooden Block> at the south end of the center row. I'll use the suggested initial position [0, -5, 5.5] for init_shift to match the example, default rotation [0,0,0], and add a note from the blueprint for identification.

The structure has been fully constructed with 33 Small Wooden Blocks arranged in a 3x11x1 grid, matching the blueprint exactly:

- Center row (x=0, y from -5 to 5, z=5.5): Blocks 1 through 11.
- East row (x=1, y from -5 to 5, z=5.5): Blocks 12 through 22.
- West row (x=-1, y from -5 to 5, z=5.5): Blocks 23 through 33.

All blocks are at the correct relative positions, forming a flat, continuous platform extending north along the y-axis (from y=-5 south to y=5 north), 3 blocks wide in x (from x=-1 west to x=1 east), and 1 block high in z. No deviations, overlaps, or errors were encountered during the build process, and the final positions confirm alignment with the blueprint's design for a static, load-bearing lower deck platform.

TERMINATE

title: builder thought
summary: Hello! I'm your engineering building assistant, specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. My goal is to collaborate with you by carefully following your instructions, using the ava…
body:
Hello! I'm your engineering building assistant, specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. My goal is to collaborate with you by carefully following your instructions, using the available tools to build step by step, and offering suggestions where helpful. We'll build one block at a time to ensure accuracy and avoid errors. The environment uses a global coordinate system where positive X points east, positive Y points north, and positive Z points upward (toward the sky).

Before we dive in, let me introduce all the tools I have at my disposal. I'll describe what each one does, what it can accomplish, and the key information I need to use it effectively. Many tools have a 'note' argument, which is very important and useful—it allows us to mark down the specific function, purpose, or identifier for a block (e.g., "Base support for upper beam"). This acts as a powerful way to reference and organize blocks conceptually, especially in complex builds.

### Available Tools:

1. **attach_block_to**: This tool attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block, expanding the structure. It's great for building chains, extensions, or adding functional components. I need the ID of the base block (as a string or integer), the face to attach to (a string like "north" or a capitalized letter—use get_block_details if unsure about face labels), the type of new block (from ['Powered Wheel', 'Small Wooden Block', 'Torch', 'Water Cannon']), and optionally a note for description. It returns a status message.

2. **connect_blocks**: This connects two existing blocks using a connector, which is useful for reinforcing structures or creating tension/compression links (but only if faces aren't too close). It accomplishes stable joins like bracing. I need IDs of both blocks, the faces to connect from/to, the connector type (from ['Brace', 'Winch']), and optionally a note. It returns a status message.

3. **remove_block**: Removes a specific block from the structure and simulation, helpful for corrections or redesigns. I just need the block's ID. It returns a status message.

4. **reset**: Resets the entire machine to an empty state, clearing all blocks. No arguments needed—useful for starting over. It returns nothing.

5. **flip_spin**: Flips the spin direction of a block (only works on spinnable blocks like wheels). Great for adjusting rotation direction in mechanisms. I need the block's ID. It returns a status message.

6. **shift_block**: Shifts a block's position by a small offset vector [x, y, z], each in [-0.5, 0.5], to fine-tune placement without detaching (e.g., to fix overlaps). I need the block's ID and the shift vector as a list of floats.

7. **twist_block**: Twists a block clockwise by an angle in degrees relative to its attached surface, useful for reorienting directional blocks (e.g., pointers or cannons). I need the block's ID and the angle (float). It returns a status message.

8. **get_block_details**: Retrieves full details of a block, including position, rotation, and face labels (which are capitalized letters). Essential for verifying attachable faces or current state before operations. I need the block's ID. It returns a string with the details.

9. **get_machine_summary**: Provides an overview of the current machine state without detailed face captions—use this for a high-level check. It's mandatory for a final verification before ending a build process. No arguments needed. It returns a string summary. (Reminder: Always use this for a final check!)

10. **start**: Initializes the build by creating and positioning the starting block (typically a base like Small Wooden Block). Accomplishes setting up the foundation at a specific location and orientation. I need an initial shift [x, y, z] as a list of floats (for position), initial rotation [yaw, pitch, roll] as a list of floats (in degrees), and optionally a note. Defaults are [0,0,0] for both if not specified. It returns a status message.

To make the most of these tools, provide clear instructions like block types, positions, faces, or notes. If something's unclear (e.g., face labels), I can suggest using get_block_details. Remember, notes help us track blocks effectively!

I see you've provided a detailed blueprint for what looks like a linear upper chord beam using Small Wooden Blocks along the y-axis for compressive support. I'll follow it step by step, building one block at a time. Let's begin by creating the starting block (block 1) using the 'start' tool, positioned at [0, -5, 7.5] with default rotation, and including the provided note.

body:
Assemble the machines following the plan {'description': 'The overall structure is a truss bridge designed to span a 10-unit gap along the y-axis. It consists of a lower deck platform that is 3 units wide in x, 11 units long in y, and 1 unit high in z, paired with an upper chord beam that is 1 unit wide in x, 11 units long in y, and 1 unit high in z. The upper chord is positioned parallel to and above the lower deck, centered in x, with a separation of 2 units in z. The two sub-structures are connected using multiple braces to form a truss, including vertical braces between aligned positions and diagonal braces between offset positions along the y-axis for structural reinforcement.', 'functionality': "The truss bridge rests its ends on the two terrains, spanning the 10-unit gap without fixed connections. The lower deck provides a wide, flat surface to support the 2.5 × 2.5 × 1.5 cargo at the center. The truss design, with the lower deck in tension, upper chord in compression, and braces handling shear forces, distributes the cargo's weight efficiently, allowing the bridge to support increasing loads until structural limits are reached.", 'assembly': 'Position the lower deck sub-structure horizontally across the gap along the y-axis. Position the upper chord sub-structure parallel and above it, centered in the x-direction, with a 2-unit separation in the z-direction. Connect them using braces: attach vertical braces between each block in the upper chord and the corresponding middle-row block in the lower deck, and add diagonal braces connecting each upper block to the adjacent lower blocks (one position ahead and behind) in the middle row.', 'motion_control': 'N/A - The structure is static and not expected to move.'}.

title: builder thought
summary: Hello! I'm your engineering building assistant, specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. My goal is to collaborate with you by carefully following your instructions, using the ava…
body:
Hello! I'm your engineering building assistant, specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. My goal is to collaborate with you by carefully following your instructions, using the available tools to build step by step, and offering suggestions where helpful to achieve the desired outcome. I'll build structures one block (or one operation) at a time to ensure precision and avoid errors. The environment uses a global coordinate system where positive x points east, positive y points north, and positive z points upward (toward the sky).

Before we dive into your truss bridge assembly plan, let me introduce all the tools I have at my disposal. I'll describe what each one does, what it can accomplish, and the key information I need from you (or that I'll infer based on the plan) to use them effectively. Remember, I can only invoke one tool per response, so we'll proceed iteratively. Also, **important note**: Several tools (like attach_block_to and connect_blocks) have an optional "note" argument. This is a powerful feature—use it to add a conceptual description or label for the new block/connector (e.g., "Vertical support brace for truss stability"). It acts as a unique identifier, making it easier to reference blocks later, track their purpose, and maintain clarity in complex assemblies.

### Assembly Tools (for adding or modifying structures):
- **add_machine**: Adds a pre-built machine (sub-structure) to the current assembly. This is great for incorporating established components like your lower deck or upper chord without rebuilding them from scratch. It allows positioning and rotating the entire sub-structure to fit the overall design. I need the machine_id (required, e.g., "5m31yqg9"), and optionally init_shift (a list of [x, y, z] floats for position offset) and init_rotation (a list of [yaw, pitch, roll] degrees). Without these, it defaults to aligning the starting block with the assembly's reference point, which could cause collisions— so specify offsets for proper placement.
- **attach_block_to**: Attaches a new single block to a specific face of an existing block. Ideal for extending or customizing structures one piece at a time (e.g., adding a wheel or torch). Available block types: ['Powered Wheel', 'Small Wooden Block', 'Torch', 'Water Cannon']. I need the base_block (ID as str or int), face (capitalized letter, e.g., "TOP"), new_block type, and optionally a note for description. Use get_block_details first if you need face info.
- **connect_blocks**: Connects two existing blocks with a connector block (like a brace or winch) between specified faces. Perfect for reinforcing structures, such as adding truss braces between your lower deck and upper chord. Available connectors: ['Brace', 'Winch']. I need block_a (ID), face_a, block_b (ID), face_b, connector type, and optionally a note. Faces must not be too close, or it fails—check distances with get_block_details.
- **shift_machine**: Moves an entire sub-machine by a position offset. Useful for fine-tuning the position of added sub-structures (e.g., aligning the upper chord precisely above the lower deck). I need the machine_index (str, from get_machine_summary) and position ([x, y, z] floats).
- **rotate_machine**: Rotates an entire sub-machine using Z-Y-X angles. Good for orienting sub-structures if the plan requires rotation. I need the machine_index (str) and rotation ([yaw, pitch, roll] degrees).

### Modification Tools (for adjusting individual blocks or components):
- **shift_block**: Slightly moves a single block by a small offset. Helpful for resolving overlaps or micro-adjustments after attachment (e.g., if a brace doesn't fit due to collision). Offsets must be in [-0.5, 0.5] per axis to avoid detachment. I need the block_id (str or int) and shift_real ([x, y, z] list).
- **twist_block**: Rotates a single block clockwise around its attachment surface. Useful for reorienting directional blocks (e.g., changing a pointer's direction). I need the block_id and angle (float in degrees). Multiple twists can achieve desired orientations.
- **flip_spin**: Reverses the spin direction of a spinning block (e.g., a wheel). Only works on spinnable blocks. I need the block_id.
- **remove_block**: Deletes a specific block from the assembly. Good for corrections. I need the block_id.
- **remove_machine**: Deletes an entire sub-machine. Useful if we need to restart a section. I need the machine_index (str).

### Utility Tools (for inspection and reset):
- **get_block_details**: Retrieves full details of a block, including position, rotation, and attachable faces (labeled with capitalized letters). Essential before attachments or connections to know valid faces. I need the block_id.
- **get_machine_summary**: Provides an overview of the current assembly state, including all blocks, their IDs, positions, and notes (without face details). Use this to check progress, get block IDs for operations, or verify before finalizing. No arguments needed. **Mandatory**: I'll always use this for a final check before considering the build complete—please remind me if needed!
- **reset**: Clears the entire assembly back to empty. No arguments. Use if we need a fresh start.

These tools allow us to build complex structures like your truss bridge efficiently. For example, we can add pre-built sub-structures with add_machine, connect them with braces using connect_blocks, and inspect with get_machine_summary. The "note" in tools like attach_block_to and connect_blocks is crucial— it helps label blocks for their role (e.g., "Diagonal truss brace"), making future references easier.

Now, let's get started on your truss bridge plan. It looks like a solid design for spanning a 10-unit gap with a lower deck (3x11x1 grid) and upper chord (1x11x1 beam), separated by 2 units in z, connected by vertical and diagonal braces. We'll use the provided candidate machines: "5m31yqg9" for the lower deck and "us7mdz5w" for the upper chord (at least one from each sub-structure, as specified).

To begin assembly:
- I'll first add the lower deck machine as the base, without any initial shift or rotation, to establish the foundation across the y-axis gap.
- Then, we'll add the upper chord above it (with a z-shift of 2 units to match the plan's separation).
- After that, we'll connect them with braces, one at a time, using block IDs from summaries and inspections.
- If needed, we can micro-adjust positions.

Let's add the lower deck first.

title: guidance message
summary: The current structure consists of the lower deck platform (sub-structure A) with 33 blocks: one starting block (A_1) and 32 small wooden blocks (A_2 to A_33), forming a 3x11x1 grid at z=5.5, spanning y from -5 to 5, wit…
body:
The current structure consists of the lower deck platform (sub-structure A) with 33 blocks: one starting block (A_1) and 32 small wooden blocks (A_2 to A_33), forming a 3x11x1 grid at z=5.5, spanning y from -5 to 5, with rows at x=-1, 0, and 1. The upper chord beam (sub-structure B) has 11 blocks: one starting block (B_1) and 10 small wooden blocks (B_2 to B_11), forming a 1x11x1 beam at z=7.5, spanning y from -5 to 5 at x=0. These are connected by 31 braces: 11 vertical braces (IDs 1-8, 11-13) aligning each upper block directly to the corresponding center-row lower block, and 20 diagonal braces (IDs 9-10, 14-31) forming crossing patterns between each upper block and the adjacent lower center-row blocks (one ahead and one behind along y, with ends connecting only inward). This configuration matches the blueprint's truss design, with the lower deck providing a flat 3-unit wide platform spanning the 10-unit y-gap, the upper chord parallel and centered above with 2-unit center-to-center z-separation, and the braces ensuring structural reinforcement for load distribution as described in the functionality. All positions align with the provided sub-structure candidates, and the assembly meets the static bridge requirements without gaps or misalignments.

TERMINATE
